text: Traditional extraction of bioactive compounds often involves the use of flammable and environmentally hazardous organic solvents. In the food and nutraceutical industries, the search for cleaner extraction methods is on-going. Natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) are recently identified green solvents formed through hydrogen bonding between natural compounds, like choline chloride, sugars and polyols. In the present work, two NADES compositions, namely betaine:triethylene glycol (Bet:TEG) and choline chloride:1,2-propanediol (Chol:Prop), were assessed for the extraction of polyphenols from spent coffee grounds (SCG), a by-product of great amount produced in the coffee industry. These NADES compositions were compared in terms of extraction yield and selectivity with commonly used green extraction methods like hot water and hydro-ethanolic extractions, while the antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the obtained extracts were also evaluated. The results showed that NADES-based extraction can achieve the same extraction yield and selectivity when compared to the green extraction methods, but with the advantages of milder temperatures and the absence of flammable substances, as well as the sustainability of the components. Furthermore, the extracts obtained with the aid of NADES compositions presented ten times higher antimicrobial activity when compared to the aqueous and hydro-ethanolic extracts. Consequently, due to their low toxicity and high effectiveness, NADES-based formulations are particularly suitable for the preparation of functional food ingredients.
explanation: The text is highly coherent and follows standard abstract conventions, which AI can produce; a minor non-native phrasing ('great amount produced') slightly lowers confidence in pure AI authorship.
